---
title: "Day 1 — Exercise: Phred quality scores"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write a function `phred_score(byte: u8) -> u8` that converts a single Phred+33 quality byte (as found in FASTQ files) to its numeric quality score. Panic if the byte is outside the valid Phred+33 range.

## Background

FASTQ files store per-base quality alongside the sequence. The quality of base *i* is the ASCII character at position *i* of the quality line. The most common encoding today is **Phred+33**: each quality score *q* (an integer in the range 0..=93) is stored as the byte *q* + 33. So:

| byte | byte value | Phred score | meaning |
|------|------------|-------------|---------|
| `!`  | 33         | 0           | as bad as it gets |
| `I`  | 73         | 40          | typical Illumina "high quality" |
| `~`  | 126        | 93          | as good as the encoding allows |

A score of *q* corresponds to a base-call error probability of 10^(−*q*/10). So *q* = 30 ≈ 1 wrong call in 1000; *q* = 40 ≈ 1 in 10,000.

![Read the strip top to bottom: pick an ASCII character, look up its byte value, subtract 33 to get the Phred score Q, then convert to an approximate error probability. The highlighted `I` column is the canonical Q = 40 Illumina "high-quality" call; `~` is the encoding's ceiling.](images/phred33-strip.svg){#fig-phred33-strip fig-alt="Six-column table with row labels on the left. Rows from top to bottom: ASCII char, byte value, subtract 33, Phred score Q, P(error). Columns from left to right: '!' byte 33 → Q 0, P(err) 1.0; '#' byte 35 → Q 2, P(err) 0.63; '5' byte 53 → Q 20, P(err) 0.01; 'I' byte 73 → Q 40, P(err) 1e-4 (highlighted in yellow with orange border); ']' byte 93 → Q 60, P(err) 1e-6; '~' byte 126 → Q 93, P(err) approximately 5e-10 (drawn in green as the ceiling)."}

Anything outside the byte range 33..=126 is not a valid Phred+33 quality and your function should panic.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day1/ex-phred
```

Make all five tests pass.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- I
```

Expected output for `I`:

```
byte 'I' -> Phred score 40
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — comparison operators on `u8`"}
[`u8`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.u8.html) supports `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `==`, `!=`. You can combine two checks with [`||`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ops/trait.BitOr.html) (logical OR):

```rust
if byte < 33 || byte > 126 {
    // out of range
}
```

For a more idiomatic version using a [range expression](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/range-expr.html), see [`RangeInclusive::contains`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/ops/struct.RangeInclusive.html#method.contains): `(33..=126).contains(&byte)`.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — panicking with a useful message"}
The [`panic!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.panic.html) macro takes the same format string syntax as [`println!`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/macro.println.html). Include the offending byte both as a number and as a character (`{:?}` on `byte as char`) so the error message is actually helpful:

```rust
panic!(
    "phred_score: byte {} ({:?}) is outside the Phred+33 range 33..=126",
    byte,
    byte as char,
);
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — the actual arithmetic"}
Once you have ruled out the out-of-range case, the score is simply `byte - 33`. Rust's [arithmetic operators](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/operator-expr.html#arithmetic-and-logical-binary-operators) on `u8` will panic on underflow in debug mode and wrap silently in release mode — so the range check is doing real safety work.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn phred_score(byte: u8) -> u8 {
    if byte < 33 || byte > 126 {
        panic!(
            "phred_score: byte {} ({:?}) is outside the Phred+33 range 33..=126",
            byte,
            byte as char,
        );
    }
    byte - 33
}
```

Full reference implementation in `day1/ex-phred-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Day 3**: replace the panic with a [`Result`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/result/enum.Result.html) — "byte out of Phred+33 range" becomes a returnable error the caller can act on, not a crash.
- **Day 4**: apply this function to every byte of every quality line in a real `.fastq.gz`, streamed through [`noodles-fastq`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/latest/noodles_fastq/), to compute the mean quality of each read.
- **Day 5**: plot the FastQC-style per-position mean-quality profile with [`plotters`](https://docs.rs/plotters/latest/plotters/).
